title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
abstract: We propose a benchmark to measure whether a language model is truthful in generating answers to questions. The benchmark comprises 817 questions that span 38 categories, including health, law, finance and politics. We tested GPT-3, GPT-Neo/J, GPT-2 and a T5-based model. The best model was truthful on 58% of questions, while human performance was 94%. Larger models were generally less truthful. This contrasts with other NLP tasks, where performance improves with model size. We suggest that scaling up models alone is less promising for improving truthfulness than fine-tuning using training objectives other than imitation of text from the web.
hallucination_rate: Best model 58% truthful (42% untruthful); human 94% truthful

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
abstract: Evaluating the factuality of long-form text generated by large language models (LLMs) is non-trivial because the text may contain a mixture of supported and unsupported pieces of information. We introduce FActScore, a new evaluation that breaks a generation into a series of atomic facts and computes the percentage of atomic facts supported by a reliable knowledge source. We conduct an extensive human evaluation to obtain FActScore annotations of people biographies generated by several state-of-the-art commercial LLMs, finding that it correlates highly with human judgments.
hallucination_rate: Varies by model; detailed atomic-level factuality scores reported
evaluation_method: FActScore (atomic fact decomposition + knowledge source verification)

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
abstract: HaluEval is a large-scale hallucination evaluation benchmark for large language models. It includes 35,000 hallucinated samples generated by ChatGPT and covers three tasks: question answering, knowledge-grounded dialogue, and text summarization. The benchmark evaluates the ability of LLMs to recognize hallucinated content.
evaluation_method: HaluEval benchmark (QA, dialogue, summarization hallucination detection)

title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection for Generative Large Language Models
abstract: SelfCheckGPT is a simple sampling-based approach to fact-check the output of black-box LLMs in a zero-resource fashion. The key idea is that if an LLM has knowledge of a given concept, sampled responses are likely to be similar and contain consistent facts. Inconsistencies across samples suggest hallucination. The method requires no external databases and works with any generative LLM.
hallucination_rate: Demonstrated effective detection via consistency-based scoring

title: HalluLens: LLM Hallucination Benchmark
abstract: HalluLens is a fine-grained benchmark evaluating intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations in large language models. It provides systematic categorization and measurement of different hallucination types across diverse tasks and model families.
hallucination_rate: Fine-grained intrinsic and extrinsic hallucination rates reported

title: Hallucination in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey, Taxonomy, and Mitigation Strategies
abstract: This paper surveys hallucination phenomena across state-of-the-art LLMs and introduces a four-tier taxonomy defining hallucinations by origin: (1) intrinsic factual contradictions, (2) extrinsic knowledge conflicts, (3) temporal reasoning failures, and (4) contextual coherence breakdowns. We evaluate five top LLMs on TruthfulQA, HaluEval, and FActScore benchmarks and find hallucination rates of 18.7% to 34.2%. We engage in a strict comparative assessment of mitigation strategies including RAG, RLHF, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and hybrid ensemble technologies. The RLHF+RAG hybrid achieved the best accuracy of 91.5%.

title: A Survey of Multimodal Hallucination Evaluation and Detection
abstract: Comprehensive survey of hallucination evaluation benchmarks and detection methods for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across Image-to-Text and Text-to-Image tasks, proposing taxonomy based on faithfulness and factuality.
hallucination_rate: Survey of reported rates across multimodal benchmarks
evaluation_method: Taxonomy of faithfulness and factuality evaluation methods

title: DefAn: Definitive Answer Dataset for LLM Hallucination Evaluation
abstract: This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark dataset consisting of over 20,000 unique prompts (more than 75,000 prompts in total) across eight domains. In experiments, nine SoTA models were tested: GPT-4o, GPT-3.5, LLama 2 7B, LLama 3 8B, Gemini 1.0 Pro, Mixtral 8x7B, Zephyr 7B, Deepseek-r1-7b, and Qwen2.5-14B. Overall factual hallucination ranges from 48% to 82% on the public dataset and 31% to 76% on the hidden benchmark. Prompt Misalignment Hallucination ranges up to 95%. Average consistency ranges from 21% to 61%.
hallucination_rate: Factual hallucination: 48%–82% (public), 31%–76% (hidden); Prompt misalignment: up to 95%
evaluation_method: DefAn benchmark (20,000+ definitive-answer prompts across 8 domains)

title: Diagnosing Untruthfulness: A G-Eval and Bootstrap Analysis of LLM Failure Modes on TruthfulQA
abstract: This study moves beyond simple accuracy metrics to systematically diagnose the error patterns of GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4 on the challenging TruthfulQA benchmark. It created an 'error corpus' by collecting incorrect responses from both models. A more advanced model, gpt-4o, was employed as a judge within a G-Eval framework to classify each error into a fine-grained taxonomy. The analysis reveals that both models exhibit stable and dominant failure modes, with the repetition of common human misconceptions being the primary cause of untruthfulness.
hallucination_rate: Dominant failure mode: repetition of common human misconceptions
evaluation_method: G-Eval framework + bootstrap simulation on TruthfulQA

title: Academic Guidebook Chatbot: Performance Comparison of Fine-Tuned Mistral 7B and LlaMA-2 7B
abstract: This study develops a local document-based chatbot using open-source AI models (Mistral 7B and LLaMA-2 7B). Fine-tuning was performed using domain-specific datasets from Academic Guidebook. Fine-tuned Mistral 7B achieved the highest METEOR value of 0.40 and F1 of 0.78 (BERTScore). Fine-tuned Mistral 7B showed faster response time than LLaMA-2. Results showed that enhancements significantly improved performance, reduced hallucinations, and enhanced response quality.
hallucination_rate: Reduced via fine-tuning; METEOR 0.40, BERTScore F1 0.78 (best model)
evaluation_method: METEOR Score + BERTScore + response time measurement

title: LongHealth: A Question Answering Benchmark with Long Clinical Documents
abstract: We present the LongHealth benchmark, comprising 20 detailed fictional patient cases across various diseases, each containing 5090 to 6754 words. The benchmark challenges LLMs with 400 multiple-choice questions in three categories: information extraction, negation, and sorting. We evaluated eleven open-source LLMs with a minimum of 16,000 tokens. Highest accuracy was observed for Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 and Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct. However, all models struggled significantly in tasks requiring identification of missing information.
hallucination_rate: All models struggled with missing information identification (clinical hallucination risk)

title: MEGA-RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework with Multi-Evidence Guided Answer Refinement for Mitigating Hallucinations of LLMs in Public Health
abstract: We propose MEGA-RAG, integrating multi-source evidence retrieval (FAISS, BM25, and biomedical knowledge graphs), a cross-encoder reranker, and a discrepancy-aware refinement module. MEGA-RAG outperforms four baseline models (PubMedBERT, PubMedGPT, standalone LLM, and LLM with standard RAG), achieving a reduction in hallucination rates by over 40%. It also achieves the highest accuracy (0.7913), precision (0.7541), recall (0.8304), and F1 score (0.7904).
hallucination_rate: 40%+ reduction in hallucination rates vs baselines
evaluation_method: Multi-evidence RAG evaluation (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, hallucination rate)

title: Battling Misinformation: An Empirical Study on Adversarial Factuality in Open-Source Large Language Models
abstract: An empirical study examining adversarial factuality in open-source large language models. Evaluates how open-source LLMs handle adversarial misinformation prompts and measures their factuality under challenging conditions.
hallucination_rate: Adversarial factuality rates reported across open-source models
evaluation_method: Adversarial prompt evaluation for factuality in open-source LLMs

title: RAC: Efficient LLM Factuality Correction with Retrieval Augmentation
abstract: RAC proposes an efficient approach to correcting factual errors in LLM-generated text using retrieval augmentation. The method focuses on post-generation factuality correction rather than prevention, providing a complementary approach to RAG-based hallucination mitigation.
models_tested: Multiple LLMs evaluated for factuality correction

title: Reducing LLM Hallucination Using Knowledge Distillation: A Case Study with Mistral Large and MMLU Benchmark
abstract: Explores using knowledge distillation to reduce hallucination in large language models. The approach transfers capabilities from a larger teacher model to a smaller student model using temperature scaling and intermediate layer matching. Testing via the MMLU benchmark demonstrated improved accuracy and reduced hallucination while remaining computationally efficient. Knowledge distillation is shown to be a practical pathway for deploying reliable language models requiring factual precision.
hallucination_rate: Improved accuracy and reduced hallucination via distillation (MMLU benchmark)
evaluation_method: MMLU benchmark evaluation + knowledge distillation comparison
